This proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Lynch's expertise in perforant path physiology with a targeted molecular dissection, focusing on the specific intersection of mGluR signaling, retrograde messengers, and Syngap1 function within a defined, behaviorally relevant circuit.

proposals.title: Single-Spine Priming of Synaptic Plasticity in the Dentate Gyrus: A Test of the Synaptic Tag-and-Capture Hypothesis at Nanoscale Resolution
proposals.motivation: Synaptic priming, where a sub-threshold event facilitates future plasticity, is a key cellular model for associative memory. While established at the cellular level, it is unknown if this priming effect operates autonomously at individual dendritic spines, a core tenet of the synaptic tag-and-capture hypothesis. Testing this requires moving beyond population-level field potential recordings to the resolution of single synapses, particularly in hippocampal circuits like the perforant path where Dr. Lynch has demonstrated novel filtering properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a priming stimulus, insufficient to induce long-term potentiation (LTP) on its own, can create a local, spine-specific 'tag' that selectively captures plasticity proteins to enable LTP from a subsequent, weak stimulus delivered to the same spine.
proposals.method: We will use two-photon glutamate uncaging (MNI-glutamate) and two-photon calcium imaging in acute mouse hippocampal slices to stimulate and monitor individual dendritic spines of dentate granule cells receiving lateral perforant path inputs. A priming protocol (weak, single-spine uncaging) will be followed by a delayed, identical weak stimulus to the same spine or a neighboring spine. LTP induction will be assessed via spine head volume dynamics and Ca2+ transients. Experiments will be conducted in wild-type and Syngap+/- mice, given SYNGAP's established role in synaptic signaling and memory, to probe molecular mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for or against spine-autonomous priming, fundamentally refining models of input-specific associative plasticity. It will bridge Dr. Lynch's expertise in perforant path signal transformation with nanoscale physiology, and reveal how Syngap haploinsufficiency disrupts this precise synaptic computation, offering mechanistic insight into related intellectual disability disorders.
proposals.novelty: While priming is studied with extracellular electrodes, directly testing its spatial specificity at the single-synapse level in a defined hippocampal pathway is novel. Combining this with a Syngap model investigates a specific molecular candidate for the 'tag' within its known neurodevelopmental context.

proposals.title: Ultrastructural Synaptic Correlates of Early-Life Stress Resilience: A Serial Electron Microscopy Analysis of CA1 Synapses Following CRF1 Receptor Blockade
proposals.motivation: Early-life stress (ELS) is a major risk factor for cognitive and psychiatric disorders, linked to hippocampal dysfunction and altered synaptic plasticity. Pre-2026 work shows corticotropin-releasing factor receptor 1 (CRF1) signaling mediates some ELS effects, and pharmacological CRF1 blockade can prevent certain behavioral and LTP deficits. However, the precise nanoscale synaptic alterations in key hippocampal circuits like the Schaffer collateral-CA1 pathway, which are critical for memory and are modulated by stress, remain unknown at the ultrastructural level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ELS induces specific, quantifiable ultrastructural pathologies at CA1 synapses (e.g., reduced spine head volume, altered postsynaptic density geometry, decreased docked vesicle pools) and that prophylactic CRF1 antagonist treatment will prevent these nanoscale alterations, providing a structural basis for functional resilience.
proposals.method: We will use serial block-face scanning electron microscopy (SBEM) on hippocampal tissue from a mouse model of ELS (maternal separation) with/without concurrent CRF1 antagonist treatment. Following perfusion and en bloc staining with heavy metals, we will image a defined stratum radiatum volume in CA1. Using pre-2026 segmentation software (e.g., Ilastik, TrakEM2 in Fiji) and convolutional neural networks (e.g., U-Net architectures) for automated synapse and organelle detection, we will perform 3D reconstructions to quantify presynaptic active zone size, postsynaptic density area, spine head volume, mitochondrial proximity, and the number of readily releasable vesicles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive 3D ultrastructural map of how ELS reshapes the synaptic architecture of a defined hippocampal circuit critical for memory. It will directly link the protective effects of CRF1 blockade to the preservation of synaptic nanostructure, moving beyond functional assays to define a structural correlate of resilience. This bridges molecular stress signaling, nanoscale anatomy, and systems-level memory function.
proposals.novelty: While ELS effects on spine density are known from light microscopy, applying high-throughput SBEM and 3D analysis to quantify sub-synaptic organelles and active zone morphology after ELS and CRF1 blockade is novel. It uniquely combines the researcher's expertise in hippocampal synaptic signaling and plasticity with cutting-edge, pre-2026 nanoscale imaging to answer a specific neurobiological question.

proposals.title: mGluR-Dependent Gating of Short-Term Plasticity Thresholds by Extracellular Divalent Cations in the Hippocampal Perforant Path
proposals.motivation: Short-term synaptic facilitation is a fundamental substrate for information processing and memory encoding, yet its regulation by extracellular Ca2+/Mg2+ is considered a passive biophysical phenomenon. Recent pre-2026 work, including from this lab, shows that microglia and specific mGluR signaling can gate distinct forms of long-term plasticity, suggesting active neuromodulatory control over plasticity thresholds. This proposal challenges the classical view by testing if mGluRs actively mediate the effects of divalent cation manipulation on facilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: Group I mGluRs are necessary for synaptic facilitation under normal divalent conditions, and their inhibition phenocopies the suppression caused by high Ca2+/low Mg2+. Conversely, activation of Group II/III mGluRs under inverted cation conditions can restore facilitation, revealing a competitive receptor-mediated switch.
proposals.method: We will perform ex vivo whole-cell recordings from dentate gyrus granule cells in mouse hippocampal slices while stimulating the lateral perforant path. Using a paired-pulse protocol, we will quantify facilitation while independently manipulating extracellular Ca2+/Mg2+ and applying specific pharmacological agents: Group I mGluR antagonists (CPCCOEt, MTEP), Group II/III agonists (LY354740, L-AP4), and a general mGluR antagonist (LY341495) as control. Experiments will follow a within-slice design to compare plasticity under normal (2mM Ca2+, 1mM Mg2+), high-Ca2+/low-Mg2+ (4mM Ca2+, 0.5mM Mg2+), and drug-applied conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, active signaling mechanism by which mGluRs gate short-term plasticity thresholds, moving beyond purely biophysical explanations. It will directly link presynaptic ion dynamics with postsynaptic and presynaptic receptor signaling into a unified regulatory model for synaptic computation, with implications for understanding circuit dynamics in learning and diseases involving SYNGAP and mGluR dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in positing that the well-documented effects of divalent cations on facilitation are not passive but are actively mediated and reversible by specific mGluR signaling pathways, a concept not explored in existing literature.

proposals.title: State-Dependent Reconfiguration of Short-Term Plasticity in the Perforant Path via Dynamic NMDA Receptor Contribution
proposals.motivation: Short-term plasticity (STP) is a fundamental synaptic filter for information flow, but its regulation by the brain's dynamic internal state is poorly understood. Pre-2026 work shows physiological variables like temperature and neuromodulators alter NMDA receptor (NMDAR) function, yet it remains unknown if these changes directly reconfigure STP rules, a critical mechanism for state-dependent memory encoding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that physiological variables (temperature, pH, neuromodulators) dynamically shift the baseline NMDAR/AMPAR contribution ratio at medial perforant path-dentate gyrus synapses, and that this shift is a primary determinant of the expression and magnitude of paired-pulse facilitation (PPF).
proposals.method: We will perform ex vivo whole-cell recordings from dentate gyrus granule cells in acute mouse hippocampal slices, stimulating the medial perforant path. Using established pharmacological isolation (APV for NMDAR, CNQX for AMPAR) and voltage-clamp protocols, we will first quantify the baseline NMDAR/AMPAR ratio under controlled manipulations of temperature (32-38°C), pH (6.8-7.4), and bath-applied neuromodulators (e.g., acetylcholine, norepinephrine). We will then, in the same synapses, measure PPF indices across these conditions and perform correlation and Granger causality analyses to test if changes in the NMDAR component predict changes in PPF.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantifiable link between the brain's physiological milieu and the computational properties of a defined hippocampal circuit. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how internal states like fever, metabolic stress, or arousal could dynamically tune synaptic filtering and signal integration, directly impacting theories of state-dependent learning.
proposals.novelty: While NMDARs are known to influence plasticity, the proposal uniquely tests the dynamic, state-dependent modulation of their contribution to baseline transmission as a direct governor of STP expression, moving beyond static receptor block studies to a systems physiology perspective.

proposals.title: Identifying the Rate-Limiting Molecular Step in Short-Term Synaptic Facilitation Through Perturbation of a Presynaptic Kinetic Model
proposals.motivation: Short-term synaptic facilitation is a fundamental form of plasticity critical for working memory and information filtering, yet its precise molecular determinants remain unclear. While known to depend on residual calcium, the specific kinetic steps within the presynaptic terminal that govern the exponential decay time constant of facilitation are not defined, limiting our ability to predict synaptic dynamics from molecular composition.
proposals.hypothesis: The exponential decay time constant of synaptic facilitation is determined by the kinetics of a specific, rate-limiting molecular step within the presynaptic calcium signaling and vesicle priming network, such as the off-rate of calcium from a high-affinity sensor like Munc13 or the disassembly of a priming complex.
proposals.method: We will construct a detailed chemical reaction network model of the presynaptic terminal, incorporating calcium influx, buffering, sensor binding, and vesicle priming steps using ordinary differential equations (ODE) solved in NEURON or MATLAB. This model will be constrained and tested by performing whole-cell recordings from dentate gyrus granule cells during lateral perforant path stimulation in acute mouse slices. We will systematically perturb the network using: 1) precise intracellular introduction of exogenous calcium buffers (e.g., EGTA, BAPTA) with known kinetics, 2) pharmacological agents targeting priming proteins, and 3) Syngap heterozygous mice, where altered signaling may modulate facilitation kinetics. Model parameters will be fit to the perturbation data using maximum likelihood estimation to identify the step whose alteration best explains shifts in the facilitation decay constant.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from a phenomenological description of facilitation to a mechanistic, predictive model anchored in specific molecular kinetics. It will identify the primary molecular determinant of facilitation decay, providing a quantitative link between protein biochemistry and short-term plasticity. The validated model will serve as a template for understanding how genetic mutations or drugs alter dynamic synaptic filtering.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying formal chemical kinetic network analysis, common in enzymology, to deconvolve the rate-limiting step in a complex neural plasticity phenomenon. It moves beyond correlating molecules with plasticity by using targeted perturbations to perform a kinetic 'sensitivity analysis' on the presynaptic machinery.

proposals.title: Direct Coupling of Presynaptic CaV2.1 Current Facilitation to Quantal Release Statistics in the Mouse Hippocampal Perforant Path
proposals.motivation: Short-term synaptic facilitation is a fundamental form of plasticity critical for information processing and working memory, yet its precise presynaptic mechanisms remain debated. Pre-2026 work, including from this lab on perforant path frequency filtering, highlights the need to directly link the biophysics of presynaptic calcium channels to the statistical parameters of neurotransmitter release. Current models, based on residual calcium or altered channel gating, lack direct, simultaneous experimental validation of this causal link.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that activity-dependent facilitation of presynaptic P/Q-type (CaV2.1) calcium currents in the lateral perforant path terminals directly drives the increase in release probability (p) observed during paired-pulse facilitation, with the kinetics of current facilitation predicting the time course of the synaptic enhancement.
proposals.method: We will perform simultaneous dual whole-cell recordings in acute mouse hippocampal slices: presynaptic voltage-clamp of identified lateral perforant path terminals (using morphological and optogenetic identification) and postsynaptic recordings from dentate gyrus granule cells. Presynaptic Ca2+ currents (isolated pharmacologically) will be evoked by paired voltage steps. Concurrently, postsynaptic quantal analysis (using variance-mean analysis and low-release probability conditions) will extract quantal parameters n and p. A custom real-time analysis pipeline will correlate the amplitude and decay kinetics of the facilitated Ca2+ current with the calculated changes in p across trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative link between facilitated presynaptic calcium influx and release probability statistics in a central synapse. It will critically test competing mechanistic models of short-term plasticity and establish a foundational framework for studying how genetic perturbations, such as in Syngap models of intellectual disability, alter presynaptic computation.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of terminal voltage-clamp with real-time quantal analysis in a defined hippocampal pathway creates a closed-loop experiment, moving beyond correlative studies to a direct, causal test of facilitation mechanisms.

proposals.title: Disambiguating the Locus of Short-Term Facilitation at CA3-CA1 Synapses: A Convergent Optical, Pharmacological, and Computational Approach
proposals.motivation: Short-term synaptic facilitation is a fundamental process underlying working memory and information filtering, yet its precise mechanistic locus—presynaptic release probability (Pr) versus postsynaptic quantal amplitude (Q)—remains ambiguous due to the core assumptions of traditional quantal analysis. This ambiguity impedes our understanding of basic synaptic computation and the interpretation of plasticity deficits in disease models like Syngap+/- mice, where altered facilitation could arise from multiple sites. A definitive, assumption-light methodology is needed to resolve this long-standing problem in synaptic physiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that short-term facilitation at the hippocampal CA3-CA1 synapse is primarily driven by a presynaptic increase in release probability, with minimal contribution from changes in postsynaptic quantal size or receptor saturation. This will be tested by applying a multi-modal framework to directly measure presynaptic calcium and vesicle release while pharmacologically clamping postsynaptic detection.
proposals.method: We will perform simultaneous whole-cell recordings and 2-photon imaging in acute mouse hippocampal slices. Presynaptic terminals will be loaded with the calcium indicator OGB-1 and the vesicular release reporter synaptophysin-pHluorin. Postsynaptic responses will be recorded in CA1 pyramidal neurons while applying the low-affinity, fast antagonist γ-DGG to assess receptor saturation. A computational model, built using the Brian2 simulator, will integrate the optical and electrophysiological data to test competing hypotheses of facilitation by simulating release statistics, calcium dynamics, and receptor binding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, multi-faceted answer to a classic question in synaptic physiology, establishing a new methodological standard for localizing plasticity. It will yield a validated computational model that can predict facilitation dynamics from measurable parameters. Furthermore, it will create a precise experimental framework for future investigations into how genetic mutations (e.g., Syngap) or circuit manipulations (e.g., perforant path stimulation) alter specific synaptic mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the convergent, adversarial application of three techniques to overcome the circularity of quantal analysis. Direct optical measurement of presynaptic function is combined with pharmacological manipulation of the postsynaptic side, and both are integrated into a single testable computational framework, moving beyond correlative or assumption-dependent approaches.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Dissection of AMPA and NMDA Receptor Contributions to Short-Term Facilitation in the Lateral Perforant Path-Dentate Gyrus Circuit
proposals.motivation: Synaptic facilitation is a key form of short-term plasticity that dynamically filters information flow in circuits like the perforant path. Pharmacological studies have implicated both AMPA and NMDA receptors, but bath-applied antagonists lack cellular specificity and can have network-wide effects, confounding interpretation of their precise roles in defined microcircuits. A method to inhibit these receptor subtypes with cell-type and temporal precision is needed to understand their distinct contributions to frequency-dependent signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AMPA and NMDA receptors on dentate granule cells make temporally distinct, non-redundant contributions to short-term facilitation at lateral perforant path synapses, with NMDA receptors specifically enabling facilitation at lower stimulus frequencies.
proposals.method: We will use Cre-driver mouse lines (e.g., Grin1-Cre for NMDA receptor-containing neurons) to achieve cell-type-specific expression of the inhibitory opsin eNpHR3.0 in dentate granule cells via AAV delivery. In acute hippocampal slices, we will record field EPSPs in the dentate gyrus molecular layer while optogenetically silencing granule cells with 589 nm light precisely timed to coincide with electrical stimulation of the lateral perforant path. We will compare facilitation ratios across a range of frequencies (5-40 Hz) under baseline conditions and during opsin-mediated, cell-specific silencing, contrasting this with effects of traditional bath-applied APV and CNQX.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel optogenetic-physiological paradigm for dissecting receptor-specific functions within a defined cell population. It will directly test the necessity of NMDA receptors on granule cells for a specific form of hippocampal short-term plasticity, providing a clearer mechanistic picture of frequency filtering in the perforant path. The approach will serve as a template for isolating synaptic receptor contributions in other complex circuits.
proposals.novelty: While optogenetics is widely used for cell-type control, its application to dissect the function of specific synaptic receptor subtypes *within* a targeted population by combining genetic targeting of receptor-expressing cells with viral opsin delivery is novel. This moves beyond the spatial and cellular limitations of bath pharmacology in studying synaptic dynamics.

proposals.title: Pharmacological Dissection of AMPA and NMDA Receptor Contributions to Short-Term Facilitation in the Lateral Perforant Path-Dentate Gyrus Circuit
proposals.motivation: The lateral perforant path (LPP) to dentate gyrus synapse exhibits unique, frequency-dependent short-term facilitation, a key filter for cortical information entering the hippocampus. Pre-2026 work by Lynch and others has characterized this filtering but the precise contributions of AMPA and NMDA receptors to this dynamic facilitation remain unresolved. Understanding this receptor-level mechanism is critical for linking synaptic signaling rules to memory encoding, especially in models of neurodevelopmental disorders like SYNGAP1 haploinsufficiency which disrupt glutamatergic signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pronounced short-term facilitation at LPP synapses is differentially supported by AMPA and NMDA receptor dynamics, with NMDA receptors providing a non-linear, voltage-dependent amplification of facilitation at higher frequencies that is distinct from canonical CA1 plasticity rules.
proposals.method: We will perform ex vivo whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from dentate gyrus granule cells in acute mouse hippocampal slices. Synaptic responses to patterned LPP stimulation (e.g., 10-40 Hz trains) will be recorded under pharmacological isolation: AMPA receptor-mediated EPSCs will be isolated using the NMDA antagonist D-AP5 (50 µM), while NMDA receptor-mediated currents will be isolated in low Mg2+ ACSF with the AMPA/kainate antagonist NBQX (10 µM). Facilitation ratios and decay kinetics will be quantified and compared between conditions. This will be complemented by a signaling analysis of paired-pulse and train-induced plasticity before and after application of selective antagonists.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct functional map of how AMPA and NMDA receptor subtypes shape a canonical hippocampal frequency filter. It will establish new timing and signaling rules for the LPP, a critical input for contextual memory. The findings will create a precise synaptic baseline for interpreting circuit dysfunctions in mouse models of memory disorders linked to glutamatergic proteins like SYNGAP1.
proposals.novelty: While receptor isolation is standard, applying it to define the mechanisms of the LPP's unique frequency filter—a system shown to have novel plasticity rules distinct from the medial perforant path and CA1—is novel. This directly tests whether the known timing rules of CA3-CA1 synapses, recently elaborated by this lab, generalize to the dentate gyrus's primary cortical input.

proposals.title: Single-Synapse Resolution of Paired-Pulse Facilitation Reveals Distinct Presynaptic Plasticity Rules in the Hippocampal Tri-Synaptic Circuit
proposals.motivation: Paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) is a canonical form of presynaptic short-term plasticity implicated in information filtering and working memory. However, bulk stimulation and recording in pathways like the perforant path or Schaffer collateral obscure synapse-specific heterogeneity. Given the distinct frequency-filtering properties of hippocampal subcircuits (e.g., Lynch's work on lateral perforant path) and the critical role of proteins like SYNGAP in synaptic function, understanding PPF at the fundamental unit of plasticity—the individual synapse—is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual synapses within a given hippocampal pathway exhibit a broad, non-uniform distribution of PPF magnitudes, and that this heterogeneity is differentially regulated by signaling pathways (e.g., SYNGAP-dependent) across circuit nodes (DG, CA3, CA1).
proposals.method: We will combine two-photon glutamate uncaging at single, visually identified dendritic spines on patched CA1 or DG granule neurons in acute mouse hippocampal slices with simultaneous whole-cell recording. Presynaptic axons will be stimulated minimally (single-fiber) using a second, targeted patch pipette or focal electrical stimulation to evoke unitary EPSCs. Paired-pulse ratios (intervals: 20-200ms) will be measured at dozens of individual synapses per pathway (lateral perforant path to DG, mossy fiber to CA3, CA3 to CA1). Data will be analyzed using custom algorithms in Igor Pro or MATLAB for deconvolution of quantal events and statistical modeling of PPF distribution variance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative map of PPF heterogeneity at single synapses in the hippocampus, linking micro-scale presynaptic properties to macro-circuit computations like filtering. It will establish a foundational dataset for modeling short-term plasticity and test how genetic perturbations (e.g., Syngap haploinsufficiency) alter the statistical rules of facilitation.
proposals.novelty: While bulk PPF is well-characterized, direct measurement at single, identified synapses with paired pre- and postsynaptic recording in defined hippocampal microcircuits has not been achieved. This approach moves beyond population averages to reveal the intrinsic diversity and circuit-specific tuning of presynaptic plasticity.

proposals.title: Mapping the Phase Transition from Facilitation to Depression at Hippocampal Synapses Using a Catalytic Resource Depletion Framework
proposals.motivation: Synaptic short-term plasticity, manifesting as paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) or depression (PPD), is a fundamental computational element in neural circuits, influencing information filtering and memory encoding. However, the precise conditions governing the transition from facilitation to depression remain poorly quantified, limiting predictive models of network dynamics. This gap is critical in circuits like the perforant path, where frequency-dependent filtering shapes hippocampal input.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition from PPF to PPD at a given synapse represents a quantifiable phase transition governed by the depletion kinetics of a finite presynaptic resource pool, analogous to site saturation in heterogeneous catalysis.
proposals.method: We will combine electrophysiological recordings in mouse hippocampal slices (focusing on lateral perforant path to dentate gyrus and CA3-CA1 synapses) with computational modeling. Experimentally, we will systematically vary stimulation patterns (frequency, duration, and initial release probability via extracellular Ca2+/Mg2+). Computationally, we will adapt Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic models from catalytic engineering, treating vesicles and release sites as a saturable resource, to fit the data and predict transition boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for predicting short-term plasticity states based on stimulation history and initial conditions. It will directly test the resource depletion model's explanatory power and yield new timing rules for synaptic integration, advancing our understanding of frequency filtering in hippocampal circuits.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct application of formal chemical kinetic models from engineering to synaptic physiology, moving beyond descriptive fits to a mechanistic, resource-constrained model of the facilitation-depression transition.

proposals.title: Correlating Synaptic Ultrastructure with Functional Recovery of Perforant Path-Dentate Gyrus Signaling Following Microglia Repopulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from this lab, shows that microglia depletion eliminates a specific form of hippocampal long-term potentiation (LTP), suggesting a critical role for microglia in synaptic plasticity. However, it remains unknown whether the functional recovery of synaptic transmission and plasticity upon microglia repopulation is directly linked to the re-establishment of morphologically normal synapses, particularly in defined pathways like the lateral perforant path. This gap limits our understanding of how structural synaptogenesis underlies functional circuit repair.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal recovery of electrophysiological function (e.g., frequency filtering and LTP) in the lateral perforant path to dentate gyrus circuit following microglia repopulation will be tightly correlated with the reappearance of synapses exhibiting normal ultrastructural morphology, including spine head size and postsynaptic density dimensions.
proposals.method: We will use a validated microglia depletion/repopulation model (CSF1R inhibitor PLX5622) in Syngap1 haploinsufficient and wild-type mice. At defined recovery timepoints, we will perform in vivo electrophysiology in the dentate gyrus to assay lateral perforant path frequency filtering and LTP. Immediately following recording, we will use serial block-face scanning electron microscopy (SBEM) of the same anatomical region to reconstruct and quantify synaptic ultrastructure (e.g., spine volume, PSD area, vesicle count) in 3D. Correlative analysis will use custom algorithms (e.g., in MATLAB) to align functional metrics (e.g., EPSP slope, paired-pulse ratio) with structural parameters across the recovery timeline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative link between the restoration of synaptic ultrastructure and the recovery of defined circuit functions after a microglia-dependent plasticity insult. It will establish whether functional recovery is gated by specific structural milestones, offering mechanistic insight into synaptic repair processes relevant to neurodevelopmental disorders like those linked to SYNGAP1.
proposals.novelty: While synaptogenesis and functional recovery are often studied separately, this proposal uniquely combines in vivo pathway-specific electrophysiology with high-resolution 3D EM in a temporally precise, perturbation-recovery model to establish a direct causal-correlative link, moving beyond associative observations.

proposals.title: Dissociating NMDA-dependent LTP from distinct forms of hippocampal-dependent learning in Syngap1 haploinsufficient mice
proposals.motivation: NMDA receptor-dependent long-term potentiation (LTP) is widely considered the primary cellular substrate for learning and memory. However, this assumption is challenged by evidence that specific forms of hippocampal plasticity, such as those in the lateral perforant path, can be NMDA-independent, and that microglia depletion eliminates a singular form of LTP without global memory impairment. This suggests learning may rely on a mosaic of plasticity mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NMDA-dependent LTP is not necessary for all forms of hippocampal-dependent learning, and that distinct learning paradigms will be differentially sensitive to the selective disruption of specific synaptic signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will use inducible, conditional Syngap1 haploinsufficient mice, where SYNGAP1 is a critical Ras/GTPase activating protein that regulates NMDA-dependent LTP. We will first confirm the loss of canonical Schaffer collateral LTP using ex vivo electrophysiology. We will then train these mice on a battery of hippocampal-dependent tasks: contextual fear conditioning (associative), spatial water maze (navigation), and pattern separation using a modified contextual discrimination task. Behavioral performance will be correlated with in vivo electrophysiological recordings of CA1 and dentate gyrus during learning, focusing on the differential engagement of medial vs. lateral perforant path inputs, to identify compensatory plasticity signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, causal test of the necessity of NMDA-LTP for diverse learning types, moving beyond correlative evidence. It will identify which memory computations are resilient or vulnerable to specific synaptic disruptions, offering a more nuanced framework for understanding neurodevelopmental disorders linked to SYNGAP1 and related signaling pathways.
proposals.novelty: While Syngap1 models are known to impair LTP and some learning, a systematic dissociation across learning types has not been established. Combining this genetic perturbation with task-specific behavioral and in vivo circuit analysis directly challenges the universality of the NMDA-LTP/learning hypothesis.

proposals.title: Spatial Learning in Mice Requires NMDA Receptor Signaling in Dentate Gyrus but Not CA1: A Site-Specific Pharmacological Dissociation
proposals.motivation: Systemic administration of the NMDA receptor antagonist AP5 impairs hippocampal-dependent spatial learning, but the precise anatomical locus of this requirement remains debated. Pre-2026 work shows NMDA receptors are critical for synaptic plasticity in both dentate gyrus (DG) and CA1, yet their specific contributions to behavior are conflated in whole-brain manipulations. Resolving this is essential for understanding the circuit-level mechanisms of memory formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NMDA receptor function in the dentate gyrus, but not in CA1, is necessary for the acquisition of spatial memory in the Morris water maze. This predicts that intra-DG, but not intra-CA1, microinfusion of AP5 will replicate the learning deficit seen with systemic administration.
proposals.method: We will use stereotaxic surgery to implant bilateral guide cannulae targeting either the dentate gyrus or CA1 in C57BL/6 mice. Following recovery, mice will receive localized microinfusions of AP5 or vehicle prior to daily training in the hidden-platform Morris water maze. Infusion sites will be verified histologically. Learning will be assessed by escape latency and probe trial performance, with statistical analysis using repeated-measures ANOVA and post-hoc tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct causal map linking NMDA receptor-dependent plasticity in specific hippocampal subfields to behavioral learning. It will clarify whether the well-established role of CA1 LTP in spatial memory reflects a necessary signaling event during acquisition or a downstream consolidation process, refining models of hippocampal information flow.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have infused AP5 into the hippocampus broadly, this proposal leverages precise anatomical targeting to dissect the unique contributions of DG and CA1 NMDA receptors to learning in real-time, moving beyond correlative electrophysiological studies to a direct behavioral test.

proposals.title: Differential Modulation of Hippocampal Synaptic Plasticity and Memory by Subunit-Selective NMDA Receptor Antagonists
proposals.motivation: The foundational role of NMDA receptors (NMDARs) in synaptic plasticity and memory is well-established, primarily through the use of broad-spectrum antagonists like AP5. However, NMDARs are heteromeric complexes with distinct GluN2 subunits (e.g., GluN2A, GluN2B, GluN2D) that confer different biophysical and signaling properties. Pre-2026 work, including the researcher's own on Syngap and signal transformations in hippocampal circuits, suggests that different NMDAR subtypes may support unique forms of plasticity and information processing. A systematic comparison of newer, subunit-selective antagonists is needed to dissect these specific contributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selective antagonism of GluN2B-containing NMDARs will impair a specific subset of long-term potentiation (LTP) paradigms and associated spatial memory tasks, distinct from the global blockade produced by AP5, revealing subunit-specific roles in hippocampal network function.
proposals.method: We will use acute hippocampal slices from adult C57BL/6 mice to compare the effects of AP5, the GluN2B-selective antagonist Ro 25-6981, and the GluN2A-preferring antagonist NVP-AAM077 on LTP. LTP will be induced at multiple synaptic pathways, including the lateral perforant path to dentate gyrus and the Schaffer collateral to CA1 pathway, using high-frequency stimulation and theta-burst protocols. In parallel, we will assess the effects of intracerebroventricular infusion of these antagonists on hippocampus-dependent spatial learning in the Morris water maze and a novel object location memory task, with careful analysis of acquisition and long-term memory recall.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly map specific NMDAR subunits to defined synaptic plasticity mechanisms and behavioral outcomes, moving beyond the monolithic view of NMDAR function. It will provide a refined pharmacological toolkit for understanding circuit-specific plasticity, with implications for disorders like SYNGAP1-related intellectual disability where NMDAR signaling is disrupted.
proposals.novelty: While AP5's effects are canonical, this study is novel in its direct, within-lab comparison of modern, subunit-selective antagonists across the same set of synaptic pathways and behavioral assays used in the researcher's prior work on frequency filtering and CA3-CA1 timing rules, allowing for a unified circuit-level interpretation of NMDAR subunit function.

proposals.title: Pharmacological Enhancement of NMDA Receptor Function Facilitates Dentate Gyrus LTP and Spatial Pattern Separation in Mice
proposals.motivation: Long-term potentiation (LTP) is a leading cellular model for learning and memory, but direct causal evidence linking enhanced LTP to improved behavioral learning is incomplete. This is a critical inverse problem: if LTP is necessary, can its potentiation be sufficient to facilitate learning? Pre-2026 work, including from this lab on perforant path frequency filtering and microglia-dependent LTP, has identified specific, modifiable synaptic loci. Testing this principle could bridge cellular physiology and cognitive enhancement.
proposals.hypothesis: Local, acute potentiation of NMDA receptor function at medial perforant path-dentate granule cell synapses will enhance a specific form of LTP and improve performance on a hippocampal-dependent spatial pattern separation task.
proposals.method: We will use in vivo electrophysiology in anesthetized mice to first establish dose-response curves for the NMDA receptor co-agonist D-serine or partial agonist D-cycloserine on LTP induced at medial perforant path synapses. Based on these results, we will infuse the optimal compound (or vehicle) bilaterally into the dentate gyrus of awake, behaving mice via implanted cannulae. Mice will then undergo training on a touchscreen-based spatial pattern separation task, which heavily relies on dentate gyrus function. Synaptic efficacy will be assayed in vivo before and after drug infusion and behavioral testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct causal test of the LTP-memory hypothesis by manipulating a specific synaptic mechanism and measuring a matched cognitive function. It will establish whether pharmacologically lowering the threshold for plasticity at a defined circuit node is a viable strategy for cognitive facilitation. The findings will inform the development of pro-cognitive therapies targeting synaptic dysfunction, as seen in conditions like SYNGAP1 haploinsufficiency.
proposals.novelty: While NMDA agonists have been tested in learning, this proposal's novelty lies in its precise circuit-level targeting (dentate gyrus, medial perforant path) and the direct linkage of a pharmacologically enhanced, physiologically verified synaptic plasticity mechanism to a computationally relevant behavioral assay of pattern separation.

proposals.title: Quantitative Autoradiographic Mapping of the Glycine B Site on NMDA Receptors in the Mouse Hippocampal Circuit
proposals.motivation: The glycine B site on the GluN1 subunit is an obligatory co-agonist site for NMDA receptor (NMDAR) activation, critically regulating synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. While NMDAR subunit composition is known to vary regionally, a detailed, quantitative map of the anatomical density of the glycine binding site itself across hippocampal subfields and connected regions is lacking. Such a map is essential for interpreting how regional variations in NMDAR co-agonist sensitivity may gate distinct forms of plasticity, like those in the perforant path and CA3-CA1 synapses studied in our lab.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anatomical density of the glycine B site on NMDARs exhibits a non-uniform distribution across hippocampal subfields and their cortical inputs, and that this distribution correlates with known regional differences in NMDAR-dependent synaptic plasticity thresholds.
proposals.method: We will use quantitative in vitro receptor autoradiography on fresh-frozen mouse brain sections. Adjacent series of sections will be incubated with 10 nM [3H]MDL-105,519, a high-affinity and selective antagonist for the glycine B site on the GluN1 subunit, with and without excess unlabeled glycine to define non-specific binding. Autoradiograms will be generated using a phosphor imager system, and optical density will be quantified in defined regions of interest (dentate gyrus, CA3, CA1, subiculum, entorhinal cortex) using ImageJ. Binding density (fmol/mg tissue) will be calculated using calibrated radioactive standards.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative atlas of glycine B site density in the mouse hippocampal formation. It will directly inform mechanistic models of synaptic signaling by linking anatomical receptor distribution to known functional heterogeneity in plasticity (e.g., lateral perforant path filtering, microglia-sensitive LTP). The data will serve as a crucial anatomical framework for future studies on Syngap and other signaling molecules that modulate NMDAR function in learning and memory circuits.
proposals.novelty: While NMDAR subunit distributions have been mapped via in situ hybridization, this proposal focuses specifically on the functional glycine co-agonist binding site using a selective radioligand, offering a direct, protein-level readout of potential co-agonist sensitivity across synapses with distinct plasticity rules.

proposals.title: In Vivo Assessment of Glycine Site NMDAR Antagonists on Seizure Burden and Ischemic Memory Deficits in Mice
proposals.motivation: NMDAR hypofunction is implicated in epilepsy and ischemic brain injury, but broad-spectrum NMDAR antagonists have severe side effects. Preclinical evidence suggests glycine site-specific NMDAR antagonists (e.g., 7-chlorokynurenic acid, L-701,324) may offer a therapeutic window by modulating receptor activity without complete blockade, yet their efficacy in integrated, whole-animal models of disease remains underexplored. Testing these compounds in vivo bridges the gap between established synaptic physiology, as studied in Dr. Lynch's prior work on hippocampal signaling and LTP, and translational behavioral outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systemic administration of glycine site NMDAR antagonists will reduce seizure severity in a kainate-induced epilepsy model and mitigate learning and memory deficits following transient global cerebral ischemia, by selectively dampening pathological NMDAR overactivation while sparing physiological synaptic function.
proposals.method: We will employ two established in vivo mouse models: intraperitoneal kainate injection for chronic epilepsy and bilateral common carotid artery occlusion for forebrain ischemia. Mice will be implanted with hippocampal electrodes for continuous EEG monitoring of seizure activity. Behavioral cohorts will undergo contextual fear conditioning and Morris water maze testing post-ischemia to assess hippocampal-dependent memory. Glycine site antagonists (7-CKA, L-701,324) will be administered systemically at varying doses pre- and post-insult. Data analysis will quantify seizure frequency/duration from EEG, memory performance from behavior, and post-mortem histology for neuronal survival, using standard statistical packages (SPSS, GraphPad Prism) for group comparisons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical in vivo efficacy data for a targeted class of NMDAR modulators, directly linking their potential neuroprotective effects to measurable behavioral and electrophysiological outcomes. It will establish whether glycine site antagonism is a viable strategy for mitigating circuit hyperexcitability and cognitive sequelae in acquired brain disorders, informing future therapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While glycine site antagonists have been studied in vitro and in acute slices, their application in chronic, behaving animal models of epilepsy and ischemia, with combined electrophysiological and behavioral readouts, is novel. This approach tests the functional consequence of a specific synaptic modulation strategy within the intact hippocampal circuit, a logical extension of Dr. Lynch's expertise in perforant path and CA3-CA1 signal transformations.

ideas.title: Test if other glutamate receptor subtypes affect LTP
ideas.description: Investigate potential roles for metabotropic glutamate receptors in the induction or expression of LTP.
ideas.sourceTitle: Contributions of quisqualate and NMDA receptors to the induction and expression of LTP.

ideas.title: AMPA/NMDA ratio increase is purely postsynaptic
ideas.description: Explore if presynaptic changes in glutamate release could also alter the AMPA/NMDA receptor response ratio.
ideas.sourceTitle: Heterosynaptic depression: a postsynaptic correlate of long-term potentiation

ideas.title: Testing the NMDA receptor as a dynamic, state-dependent switch between LTP induction and expression phases
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that subtle, activity-dependent modifications to the NMDA receptor complex itself—such as changes in subunit composition, phosphorylation state, or allosteric modulator binding—are the critical molecular events that functionally separate and then link the induction and expression phases of LTP. We hypothesize that the induction protocol triggers a specific, persistent alteration in NMDA receptor properties (e.g., a shift in conductance, calcium permeability, or coupling to intracellular signaling), which then acts as a permissive switch, enabling the sustained expression of LTP while potentially making the synapse refractory to further induction. This directly tests if the 'separable' processes of induction and expression are in fact coordinated through the dynamic reconfiguration of the very receptor class thought to be unchanged, offering a unified mechanism for synaptic metaplasticity.

ideas.title: Investigate mGluR-dependent modulation of synaptic facilitation through inverted divalent cation manipulation
ideas.description: This study proposes that the suppression of synaptic facilitation by raising extracellular Ca2+ or lowering Mg2+ is not merely a passive biophysical effect but is actively gated by metabotropic glutamate receptor (mGluR) activity. We will test the hypothesis that Group I mGluRs (mGluR1/5) are necessary for the expression of facilitation under normal conditions and that their inhibition mimics the suppressive effects of high Ca2+/low Mg2+. Conversely, we will investigate if Group II/III mGluR activation under inverted cation conditions can paradoxically restore facilitation, revealing a competitive receptor-mediated switch controlling short-term plasticity thresholds. This approach directly couples the presynaptic ion concentration manipulation of Idea A with the postsynaptic and presynaptic receptor signaling of Idea B to uncover a unified regulatory mechanism.

ideas.title: Test other NMDA antagonists on LTP and learning
ideas.description: Compare AP5's effects with newer, more selective NMDA receptor antagonists.
ideas.sourceTitle: Selective impairment of learning and blockade of long-term potentiation by an N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonist, AP5

ideas.title: Use patch clamp with selective channel blockers to isolate receptor currents during LTP
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological approach from cardiac electrophysiology to neuroscience. In cardiac studies, isolating a specific ionic current (like I_Ca,L) amidst a milieu of others is achieved by voltage clamping a cell while applying a precise combination of pharmacological blockers to eliminate all other major currents. We adapt this by applying whole-cell voltage clamp to a neuron during the induction of LTP. To directly and cleanly measure the current through either NMDA or non-NMDA (AMPA/kainate) receptors, we will use a protocol of selective pharmacological isolation. When measuring the NMDA receptor current, AMPA/kainate receptors will be blocked with CNQX (and vice-versa with AP5 for NMDA receptors when measuring AMPA currents), while also using standard blockers for GABAergic and other synaptic inputs. This allows for the direct observation of the kinetics and magnitude of the receptor-specific current flux during the plastic event, minimizing contamination from other parallel conductance changes.

ideas.title: Priming effect requires NMDA receptor activation
ideas.description: Test if the priming effect is independent of NMDA receptors, unlike the subsequent potentiation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Induction of synaptic potentiation in hippocampus by patterned stimulation involves two events.

ideas.title: Test TrkB receptor localization and role
ideas.description: Confirm postsynaptic TrkB receptor involvement and its necessity for the observed BDNF-dependent LTP.
ideas.sourceTitle: Heterosynaptic depression: a postsynaptic correlate of long-term potentiation

ideas.title: Characterize the kinetics of LTP expression after DNQX washout using a pharmacological 'cage-and-release' paradigm
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'cage-and-release' paradigm from photopharmacology to characterize the hidden kinetics of LTP expression. In the original synaptic experiment, LTP is induced in the presence of DNQX, which blocks AMPA receptors and masks potentiation; washout then 'releases' the system to express LTP. The analogous photopharmacological solution uses rapid, precise activation. Adapted here, the research would employ ultra-fast microfluidic perfusion systems to achieve millisecond-scale, complete DNQX washout at a precise synaptic locus. This would be combined with high-temporal-resolution readouts (e.g., two-photon glutamate uncaging EPSPs, spine-specific Ca2+ imaging, or dendritic patch-clamp) to measure the latency, rate, and stabilization phase of the potentiation signal from the moment of AMPAR disinhibition. This approach moves beyond confirming that LTP is revealed to defining the exact kinetic signature of its expression, distinguishing between rapid post-translational modifications and slower trafficking events.

ideas.title: Mapping the molecular kinetics of synaptic facilitation to a chemical reaction network model
ideas.description: This research idea applies the problem-solving framework from enzyme kinetics and metabolic network modeling to the neuroscience question of what determines the exponential decay constant of synaptic facilitation. In the analogous field, the temporal dynamics of a biochemical system are solved by modeling it as a network of coupled reactions and identifying the rate-limiting steps. Adapted here, the presynaptic terminal during facilitation is treated as a chemical reaction network involving calcium influx, binding to various sensors and buffers, and the subsequent priming of vesicles. The solution involves constructing a kinetic model of this network, then using targeted experimental perturbations—such as introducing calcium buffers with precisely known kinetics, using pharmacological agents, or employing genetic mutations that alter specific protein-protein interaction rates—to vary parameters. By measuring how these perturbations shift the facilitation decay time constant and fitting the data to the model, the research will identify the specific molecular actors (e.g., the off-rate of calcium from a particular high-affinity site on Munc13, or the disassembly rate of a priming complex) whose kinetics are the primary determinants of the observed exponential decay time.

ideas.title: Dynamic modulation of NMDA receptor contribution to short-term plasticity by physiological variables
ideas.description: This study investigates how physiological variables (temperature, pH, neuromodulators) dynamically alter the proportion of the baseline synaptic response mediated by NMDA receptors, and how this shift in receptor contribution subsequently governs the expression and magnitude of paired-pulse facilitation. By precisely quantifying the NMDA component under varied conditions and correlating it with facilitation indices, the research will uncover whether changes in the NMDA/AMPA ratio serve as a fundamental mechanism through which the brain's internal state tunes short-term synaptic plasticity. This approach moves beyond static measurements to reveal how the synaptic 'filter' for signal transmission is continuously reconfigured by the physiological milieu.

ideas.title: Use optogenetic isolation of cell-type-specific contributions to facilitation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from circuit neuroscience, where optogenetic tools are used to isolate the function of specific neuronal cell types within a network. The analogous problem of dissecting distinct contributions is solved by using genetically targeted light-sensitive proteins to control defined populations with high temporal precision. Adapted to the original research context, this involves using Cre-driver lines for receptor subunit genes combined with viral delivery of inhibitory opsins to achieve cell-type- and receptor-subtype-specific inhibition. This allows for the dissection of AMPA vs. NMDA receptor contributions to synaptic facilitation with superior cellular and temporal specificity compared to bath-applied pharmacological antagonists, which affect all receptors of that subtype in the tissue.

ideas.title: Optically triggered revelation of silent LTP using a photo-reversible AMPAR antagonist to define true expression kinetics
ideas.description: This study uses a photo-reversible AMPAR antagonist to induce and maintain LTP in a pharmacologically isolated 'silent' state, enabling the precise optical uncaging of the antagonist to restore AMPAR transmission with millisecond precision. The instantaneous switch from blocked to functional AMPARs allows the measurement of the basal synaptic response and its potentiated state in rapid succession, capturing the true initial kinetics of LTP expression without the confounding plasticity introduced by a prolonged chemical washout. This approach is combined with real-time electrophysiological and optical monitoring to confirm that the observed potentiation is the direct revelation of the pre-established LTP engram, not a new form of plasticity triggered by the revelation process itself.

ideas.title: Mapping the Dependency Matrix of LTP Induction and Expression
ideas.description: This project systematically maps the interdependencies between LTP induction and expression mechanisms across molecular, cellular, and circuit levels. Using a closed-loop experimental platform in awake, behaving rodents, we will precisely trigger induction events via optogenetic stimulation of specific pathways while simultaneously monitoring expression signatures through real-time calcium imaging and dendritic spine morphology tracking. The core innovation is treating induction and expression as a multi-dimensional dependency matrix, measuring how perturbations to specific induction components (e.g., NMDA receptor subtypes, calcium sources) alter specific expression components (e.g., AMPA receptor trafficking, structural changes, circuit-level potentiation). This approach moves beyond the untestable question of complete separability to generate a functional map of their necessary linkages.

ideas.sourceTitle: Contributions of quisqualate and NMDA receptors to the induction and expression of LTP.

ideas.title: VIP may also be expressed in principal excitatory cells
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that VIP is expressed exclusively in hippocampal interneurons.
ideas.sourceTitle: Brief bursts of high-frequency stimulation produce two types of structural change in rat hippocampus.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Tracking from Immunology to Map AMPA/Kainate Receptor Dynamics Post-LTP
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from immunology, where single-molecule tracking (e.g., PALM) was used to visualize the dynamic reorganization of T-cell receptors post-antigen engagement, to solve the parallel problem in neuroscience of identifying specific alterations in non-NMDA (AMPA/kainate) receptors underlying LTP. The analogous solution involved quantifying changes in receptor mobility and clustering at the nanoscale. The adapted research idea is to employ these single-molecule super-resolution techniques to track labeled AMPA/kainate receptor subunits in live neurons, directly measuring and comparing their diffusion, confinement, and nanoscale organization in the postsynaptic membrane before and after LTP induction. This would move beyond bulk measurements to visualize the precise dynamic molecular changes—such as receptor immobilization or incorporation into nanodomains—that constitute the expression of LTP.

ideas.title: Nanoscale reorganization of NMDA receptor composition and mobility during LTP expression at single synapses
ideas.description: This project directly tests the hypothesis that LTP expression involves a nanoscale reorganization of NMDARs at the potentiated synapse, which could alter channel properties without changing the overall number. We will induce LTP at single dendritic spines using 2-photon glutamate uncaging while performing dual whole-cell recordings to isolate and quantify NMDAR-mediated currents. In parallel, we will use super-resolution imaging to map the pre- and post-synaptic nanoscale architecture of NMDAR subunits (GluN1, GluN2A/B) and PSD-95, and employ single-particle tracking of quantum dot-labeled NMDARs to measure changes in receptor confinement and diffusion specifically within the potentiated spine. This integrated approach is designed to detect subtle, functionally consequential modifications to the NMDAR complex that are spatially restricted to the site of plasticity.

ideas.title: Quantal-Voltage-Clamp Analysis of Presynaptic Facilitation
ideas.description: This fused approach combines simultaneous presynaptic voltage-clamp recording of calcium currents with postsynaptic quantal analysis of synaptic transmission during facilitation. By precisely controlling and measuring the presynaptic calcium influx while statistically analyzing the changes in quantal parameters (m, n, p), the experiment can directly test if the magnitude and kinetics of the facilitated calcium current predict the observed increase in release probability. This allows for a mechanistic dissection of facilitation, distinguishing between models based purely on residual calcium from those involving altered calcium channel gating or sensor affinity. The synergy creates a closed-loop experimental framework where presynaptic biophysics and postsynaptic statistics are linked in real time within the same preparation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Clamp-Based Isolation of NMDAR Current Under Physiological Voltage Trajectories
ideas.description: This approach precisely quantifies the NMDAR contribution to baseline transmission by preserving the native postsynaptic voltage environment. While blocking AMPARs pharmacologically, a conductance-based dynamic clamp system injects a simulated AMPAR current derived from a model fitted to the neuron's own pre-block response. This maintains the natural membrane potential fluctuations, including the moment-to-moment relief of Mg2+ block that occurs during real synaptic potentials. The concurrently recorded, isolated NMDAR current thus reflects its true contribution under physiological conditions, free from artifacts caused by holding potential changes or altered input resistance.

ideas.title: A multi-modal framework to disambiguate presynaptic and postsynaptic contributions to synaptic facilitation
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond standard quantal analysis to definitively localize the site of facilitation. It employs a triad of complementary techniques: 1) direct optical measurement of presynaptic calcium dynamics and vesicle release, 2) pharmacological manipulation of postsynaptic receptor saturation and quantal amplitude, and 3) a computational model that integrates these data to test mechanistic hypotheses. This approach overcomes the core assumptions and ambiguities of traditional quantal analysis, providing convergent evidence to distinguish presynaptic increases in release probability from postsynaptic changes in quantal size or detection.

ideas.title: Investigate the Phase Transition from Facilitation to Depression via Resource Depletion Models
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from chemical engineering, specifically the modeling of phase transitions in catalytic activity, to investigate the inverse transformation from paired-pulse facilitation to depression at synapses. In catalysis, a shift from reaction facilitation to inhibition is understood through kinetic models of site saturation and resource depletion (e.g., Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanisms). The adapted idea applies this framework to synaptic function by modeling the readily releasable pool and release sites as a finite catalytic resource. By systematically varying presynaptic stimulation patterns and initial release probability, the study will map the precise conditions—such as specific frequencies and durations that lead to vesicle pool depletion or release site inactivation—that force a phase transition from facilitation to depression. This approach provides a quantitative, model-driven method to understand the scaling duality of synaptic plasticity.

ideas.title: Mechanistic dissection and computational modeling of the dynamic boundary between synaptic facilitation and depression
ideas.description: This study moves beyond descriptive correlation to establish a causal, mechanistic understanding of how synapses switch from facilitation to depression. We combine dynamic clamp and paired recordings with a biophysically detailed computational model of the presynaptic terminal to form a closed-loop hypothesis-testing platform. The model, constrained by initial physiological data, proposes specific manipulations (e.g., altering calcium buffering, priming vesicle pools) and predicts outcomes for complex stimulation patterns, which are then executed experimentally. This iterative cycle identifies the minimal sufficient conditions and key molecular players that deterministically control the facilitation-depression transition, yielding predictive rules rather than a mere catalog of correlations.

ideas.title: Cross-linking stabilized autoradiography for mapping immobilized receptor pools after LTP
ideas.description: This method combines tissue cross-linking with quantitative receptor autoradiography to specifically map changes in the immobilized receptor pool following LTP induction. Perfusion with a membrane-impermeant cross-linker is performed immediately after LTP induction to covalently stabilize protein complexes, including synaptic receptors, in their in vivo state. Brain sections are then processed for autoradiography using selective radioligands under saturating conditions on both cross-linked and non-cross-linked paired slices. This dual approach allows the differentiation of total receptor protein localization from ligand-binding-competent pools, providing a more robust map of receptor trafficking versus allosteric modulation.

ideas.title: Test linear summation of facilitation using a pharmacokinetic compartment model approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to predicting multi-dose drug concentrations from pharmacokinetics to test the linear summation model of synaptic frequency facilitation. In pharmacokinetics, the principle of superposition solves the analogous problem by summing the exponential decay curves from individual doses, assuming a linear, one-compartment system. The adapted research idea is to experimentally validate the linear summation model by treating each stimulus in a train as inducing a facilitatory event with exponential decay. The recorded facilitation will be compared to the predicted sum of these individual decays. This approach allows for quantitative fitting to extract parameters like the facilitation decay time constant and provides a rigorous statistical framework to test for significant non-linear interactions between facilitatory events, which would violate the simple linear model.

ideas.title: Decompose facilitation decay kinetics into constituent molecular mechanisms
ideas.description: This project rejects the assumption of a single decay constant and instead treats the facilitation decay curve as a composite signal. The research system first kinetically dissects the decay using advanced electrophysiological protocols and analysis to resolve distinct exponential components. It then employs a combination of pharmacological, genetic, and optical interventions to selectively perturb candidate molecular processes, assigning each resolved kinetic component to specific actors like residual Ca2+ clearance systems, sensor saturation states, or autoregulatory feedback loops. The final output is a mechanistic map showing which molecular processes dominate at different time points post-activity, explaining the shape of the decay.

ideas.title: A High-Throughput Microfluidic Platform for Mapping Synaptic Plasticity Across Multidimensional Physiological Space
ideas.description: This project adapts a high-throughput screening solution from chemical engineering catalysis research to the study of synaptic plasticity. In catalysis, microreactor arrays are used to map catalyst efficiency across a multidimensional parameter space (temperature, pressure, concentration) in parallel. Adapted for neuroscience, the idea is to design a microfluidic neural culture platform with multiple independent chambers where temperature, pH, and neuromodulator levels can be precisely and combinatorially controlled. Using this system, paired-pulse facilitation can be assayed simultaneously across all these varied physiological conditions, enabling the efficient construction of a detailed functional map that reveals how these factors interact to modulate short-term synaptic plasticity, moving beyond one-variable-at-a-time experiments.

ideas.title: Closed-loop model falsification to isolate novel synaptic integration in high-frequency trains
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond descriptive observation of non-linearity to actively test for mechanisms not captured by canonical models of short-term plasticity. A high-resolution biophysical model, constrained by low-frequency dynamics, generates a null prediction for sustained high-frequency output. The core experiment is a real-time, closed-loop comparison where the predicted synaptic current is injected into the postsynaptic neuron concurrent with the actual presynaptic train, allowing direct measurement of any discrepancy. This residual signal, which cannot be explained by facilitation, depression, or depletion, is the definitive signature of a unique, non-linear integrative mechanism.

ideas.title: Examine paired-pulse facilitation using high-throughput, single-vesicle imaging adapted from single-molecule biophysics
ideas.description: The original idea to investigate presynaptic and postsynaptic mechanisms of paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) is analogous to the problem in enzymology of understanding heterogeneous reaction mechanisms hidden by ensemble averaging. That field was revolutionized by high-throughput single-molecule fluorescence assays. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to examine PPF at the subcellular level using high-throughput, single-vesicle imaging techniques. By employing super-resolution microscopy and single-vesicle fluorescent reporters (e.g., vG-pHluorin), one can directly observe the fate of individual synaptic vesicles—their release probability, fusion kinetics, and re-priming dynamics—in response to paired stimuli. This approach will resolve whether PPF arises from increased vesicle release probability, an increase in the readily releasable pool, or changes in fusion pore properties, providing a mechanistic map at the ultimate resolution of the synaptic transmission process.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Processing to Isolate Synaptic Plasticity Rules During Natural Behavior
ideas.description: The original idea aims to scale the study of paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) rules from reduced preparations to the in vivo, behaving animal, a context dominated by complex, overlapping neural activity. The analogous problem in geophysics is isolating a specific subsurface signal from pervasive ambient seismic noise. That field solved it with seismic interferometry, using multi-sensor arrays and correlation techniques to extract clean impulse responses from noise. Adapting this, the research idea is to deploy high-density neural recordings in an animal learning a task and use computational interferometry-inspired methods. By analyzing the correlations in spontaneous and evoked activity across many recorded synapses over long time periods, we can attempt to back out the core PPF learning rules that persist amidst, and potentially drive, the behavioral dynamics.

ideas.title: Leveraging Network Resonance to Enable Heterosynaptic Facilitation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the strict homosynaptic specificity of facilitation by drawing an analogy from wireless communication networks, where constructive interference from multiple, synchronized sources can amplify a signal at a receiver. The analogous solution involves precise spatiotemporal alignment of inputs to create resonant amplification. Adapted to neuroscience, the research would test if heterosynaptic inputs can show facilitation under specific temporal or spatial conditions that cause their subthreshold effects to summate constructively, potentially through mechanisms like dendritic spike initiation or voltage-dependent conductance activation, thereby violating the canonical rule that facilitation is exclusive to the repeatedly activated synapse itself.

ideas.title: Test domain-specific validity of linear summation under non-exponential facilitation decay
ideas.description: This research systematically tests the linear summation model of residual facilitation across a wide range of inter-stimulus intervals, explicitly probing intervals where facilitation decay is known or suspected to deviate from a simple first-order exponential. By combining high-resolution experimental protocols with kinetic modeling, the study aims to identify the specific temporal domains (e.g., very short or long intervals) where linear summation breaks down. This will reveal whether the model's validity is contingent on the underlying decay kinetics, providing a more nuanced understanding of synaptic computation and potentially leading to revised, context-dependent models of short-term plasticity.

ideas.title: Test synaptic facilitation decay kinetics using stretched exponential and multi-exponential models from polymer physics
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that synaptic facilitation decay universally follows first-order exponential kinetics. Drawing an analogy from polymer physics, where the relaxation of disordered materials required more complex models like the stretched exponential to account for heterogeneity, we adapt this solution to neuroscience. The adapted idea is to test if facilitation decay is better described by a stretched exponential or multi-exponential model, which would imply a distribution of underlying presynaptic processes or states rather than a single homogeneous mechanism. This involves collecting high-resolution paired-pulse facilitation data across extreme intervals and performing comparative model fitting to move beyond the oversimplified single-exponential assumption.

ideas.title: Apply electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to isolate presynaptic membrane dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from electrochemistry and corrosion science, where electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to deconvolve overlapping interfacial processes by analyzing the frequency-dependent impedance response to a small AC voltage perturbation. The analogous solution is adapted to the neuroscience context by applying a sinusoidal voltage-clamp command to the presynaptic terminal across a spectrum of frequencies. The resulting complex impedance analysis would allow researchers to isolate the presynaptic calcium current from other overlapping capacitive and ionic currents based on its unique kinetic (frequency) signature, providing a more refined measurement of calcium influx dynamics during synaptic facilitation.

ideas.title: Causal dissection of short-term facilitation as a control parameter for gamma rhythm generation and stability
ideas.description: This project causally tests the hypothesis that short-term facilitation in specific cortical microcircuits acts as a tunable control parameter for gamma oscillations. Using a combined computational and experimental approach, we first model how manipulating facilitation kinetics in a canonical pyramidal-interneuron network alters oscillation frequency, power, and robustness. We then validate these predictions experimentally by using closed-loop optogenetic stimulation of facilitating excitatory pathways onto interneurons in brain slices, directly measuring how engineered facilitation shapes emergent population rhythms. This mechanistic framework moves beyond correlation to establish facilitation as a dynamic determinant of oscillatory network states.

ideas.title: Deconvolving Population Signals with Single-Source Validation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that population field potentials accurately represent uniform changes at individual synapses during LTP by drawing an analogy to cosmology. In astrophysics, the diffuse Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) signal is deconvolved and validated against observations of individual, well-understood sources (like galaxy clusters) to interpret what the integrated signal means for local physics. Adapting this solution, the research would develop deconvolution models to infer synaptic-level changes from field potential data and then critically test these models by simultaneously recording from identified single synapses during the same LTP induction. This direct validation approach aims to define the precise relationship and potential discrepancies between the population-level readout and the underlying synaptic heterogeneity, determining when the field potential is a reliable proxy for uniform synaptic change.

ideas.title: Apply Poisson Failure Analysis from Telecommunications to Synaptic Facilitation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from telecommunications network reliability. In that field, Poisson failure analysis of bit-error rates is used to diagnose whether signal degradation is presynaptic (at the transmitter) or postsynaptic (in the transmission channel). We adapt this solution by modeling synaptic transmission as a communication channel, where a release site is a transmitter and a failure to release a vesicle is a 'bit error'. By applying quantal analysis—specifically examining the variance and mean of synaptic failures during facilitated responses—we can perform a statistical test. If the data conform to a Poisson process where the release probability is the modulating parameter, it provides strong evidence that the facilitation mechanism is presynaptic, as the statistical properties are inherent to the quantal release process itself.

ideas.title: Use structured illumination and optical flow analysis to map synaptic facilitation dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from atmospheric science, specifically particle image velocimetry (PIV) used for mapping wind flow fields. In meteorology, tracer particles are illuminated with structured light, imaged at high speed, and displacement between frames is analyzed to compute flow vector fields. Adapted to neuroscience, voltage-sensitive dye fluorescence in a hippocampal slice serves as the dynamic tracer signal representing membrane potential changes. By using programmable structured illumination (e.g., with a digital micromirror device) to excite specific tissue planes or patterns sequentially, and then applying optical flow analysis to the high-speed optical imaging data, one can generate spatial vector maps of facilitation spread. This reveals not just where facilitation occurs, but the direction and speed of the depolarization wavefront, providing a dynamic spatial map of synaptic plasticity propagation.
